decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing / enterprise IT infrastructure / consumer products and hospitality
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred potential downstream deal size: approximately $15M-$50M over 3-5 years, based on enterprise-scale cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting evaluation for Kohler's Wisconsin data center, large VMware estate, significant physical server footprint, SAP ECC/BW/CRM/SCM/EWM/SRM/GRC/Fiori/BPC/BusinessObjects landscape, Windows/Linux servers, databases, backup/DR, networking, monitoring, logging, security tooling, and SAP/non-SAP cost modeling. Current RFP itself is Phase 0 evaluation and does not constitute a commitment.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. The document frames this as a Phase 0 evaluation/business-case RFP, with detailed contractual terms and service delivery frameworks to be finalized after vendor selection. Inferred downstream hosting/commercial scenarios should likely be modeled over a 3-5 year horizon, but no binding contract term is stated in the provided text.

requirements.text: Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; RFP responses/proposals are due to Kohler on 26 June.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A late submission or use of the wrong communication channel can prevent the proposal from being considered before evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The document explicitly says simple Yes/No answers will not be sufficient, making response-template compliance a gate.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Certification evidence is mandatory but the certification set is unspecified, creating a hidden compliance gate likely detailed in the separate Vendor Response Template.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is conditional on being a DCaaS provider and is easy to miss because it appears in the compliance subsection.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Industry standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers; specific attestations not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency requirements are often treated as administrative compliance gates and can invalidate pricing if missed.

requirements.text: Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Using the wrong email path may conflict with the designated communication-channel requirement.

requirements.text: All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The separate template may contain additional mandatory fields not visible in the main document.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears as a required response component, but the document does not state the required jurisdiction; vendors must still address it explicitly.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required; no specific residency country/state stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios (Cloud, DCaaS, Hybrid) where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler's enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
Proposed models should demonstrate how they can support a progressive transition away from the current Data Center footprint while maintaining business continuity and operational stability.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory baseline is a required sizing input and is outside the provided main document.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Failure to call out data gaps could undermine pricing and solution validity.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory spreadsheet is required for sizing, design, and pricing but is not included in the text provided for analysis.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
The in-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.

The in-scope services include commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications, Windows and Linux virtual machines, databases, middleware and integration platforms, file storage systems, backup and disaster recovery solutions, IAM integrations, network connectivity, monitoring, logging and security tooling.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only when limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workloads are limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios; over-scoping OT integration could be noncompliant.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning and execution activities, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP explicitly excludes execution work; proposing full execution as the core response may be misaligned.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities such as code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Modernization/remediation services are excluded from this Phase 0 scope unless only addressed indicatively.

requirements.text: Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Only high-level solution design/reference architecture is expected; detailed LLD is excluded.

requirements.text: Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Full steady-state managed services commitments are excluded from the Phase 0 scope, despite pricing requesting possible managed services cost visibility.

requirements.text: Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Vendors are only required to demonstrate readiness, not execute audits/certifications in this phase.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration / implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: SAP cost visibility is specifically required and can be missed if pricing is presented only as an aggregate hosting estimate.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler expects pricing protections and efficiency gains over time, which may conflict with standard cloud/service-provider escalation models.

requirements.text: Cost increase protections and efficiency gains should be based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP specifies that protections should be based on actual prices being paid, not headline discount percentages.

requirements.text: Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a commercial trap: anything below net 90 is explicitly non-competitive, even though not phrased as automatic rejection.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Payment terms should be no less than net 90.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.
The governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model.
The governance overview should include reporting and review mechanisms such as periodic status updates and performance tracking.
The governance overview should include an escalation framework for issue resolution.
The governance overview should include key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This restriction can affect use of subcontractors, affiliates, hyperscaler support teams, or offshore solution teams unless prior written consent is obtained.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is buried in the appendix and can block normal partner/subcontractor sharing without Kohler's prior written consent.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This appendix requirement restates broad compliance capability across enterprise IT and manufacturing environments, but exact obligations are deferred.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations are not specifically listed.

similar_deals.outcome: Past proposal content; outcome not provided

Outcome is not provided, so it must not be treated as a proven win.

similar_deals.key_lesson: Fast mobilisation and sector credentials won the deal. For Kohler, commit to mobilisation within two weeks and lead with manufacturing uptime, plant continuity, SAP, and hybrid infrastructure credibility.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 42%. Evidence supports a credible but not favored bid. Positive evidence: there is an exact-match Kohler Project Polaris past proposal pattern with strong alignment to the RFP; the proposed 12-week fixed-price Hosting Decision Lab at $279,533.42 and downstream 36-month model at $30,667,392 are well matched to the inferred Phase 0 and $15M-$50M downstream opportunity; DEAL-089 shows a manufacturing IT transformation win at $22M driven by rapid mobilisation and sector credentials; DEAL-003 shows SAP-heavy manufacturing credibility can beat Accenture/Deloitte; and DEAL-011/DEAL-007 show comparable references and accelerators can beat Cognizant-type competitors. Negative evidence: there is no public procurement data available, so pricing and competitor award trends cannot be validated against ground truth; the Kohler-specific proposal record has no stated outcome and therefore cannot be counted as a win; Cognizant appears to have incumbent/strategic-partner presence across relevant operational towers, which materially lowers probability; the RFP has multiple eliminatory compliance, template, attestation, communication-channel, and USD-pricing gates; and DEAL-056 shows manufacturing bids can be lost on appendix-level compliance misses while DEAL-029 shows better technical bids can lose on price. Confidence is medium-low because the assessment uses strong past-proposal and deal-corpus evidence but lacks public procurement award validation.
capability_gaps: Current evidence gap against eliminatory compliance requirement: the bid must include evidence of certifications for hosting workloads in applicable industry/regional standards; certifications are referenced in prior deal patterns, but the RFP response still needs a Kohler-specific certification evidence matrix covering hyperscaler, DCaaS, security, backup/DR, monitoring/logging, and operating locations.

Current evidence gap against DCaaS eliminatory requirement: if proposing DCaaS, partner/provider compliance attestations must be explicitly supplied; these attestations are not confirmed in the available intelligence and must be obtained before submission.

Current response-completeness gap against mandatory template/detail requirement: the RFP requires detailed completion of Kohler’s Vendor Response template and simple Yes/No answers will not be accepted; the available proposal intelligence provides strong narrative content but does not confirm final template population or scoring alignment.

Current commercial-model gap against USD pricing and business-case requirement: Phase 0 pricing is available at $279,533.42 and downstream pricing at $30,667,392, but Kohler likely needs scenario-based 3–5 year TCO options across retained DC, DCaaS, public cloud, hybrid, SAP/non-SAP split, migration, run, licensing, and support costs.

Current architecture-detail gap against target-state requirements: the available proposal pattern references Azure-first landing zone, hybrid guardrails, identity, security, and governance, but the final response must explicitly map to multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone principles, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity-provider integration, landing-zone deployment model, and application deployment model.

Current client-access gap against credible Phase 0 deliverables: a defensible workload-by-workload placement model requires Kohler inventory, SAP/VMware/physical server details, cost baselines, RTO/RPO, network dependencies, and security tooling data; the intelligence flags no-bid risk if templates/inventory and stakeholder workshops are not secured.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Factory: position Project Polaris as an evidence-led workload placement decision across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS—not as a forced cloud-first migration. This directly matches the strongest Kohler-specific proposal pattern.

Manufacturing-safe transformation with zero-disruption governance: emphasize plant/business continuity, SAP uptime, rollback gates, DR test blueprint, resilience tiers, and fee-at-risk governance tied to application availability. This follows DEAL-089’s manufacturing-domain win pattern and DEAL-003’s SAP low-downtime lesson.

Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: provide scenario-based 3–5 year economics for cloud, DCaaS, retained DC, hybrid, SAP/non-SAP, licensing, migration, run, and support. This mitigates DEAL-029’s loss lesson where a better solution lost due to pricing being 20% above the winner.

Compliance-first proposal discipline: make certifications, attestations, hosting locations, data residency, security controls, RFP template compliance, communication channel, and USD pricing visible as executive-level proof points. This directly addresses DEAL-056, which was lost on a missed compliance appendix requirement.

Rapid mobilisation plus accelerators: commit to a two-week mobilisation and use named accelerators for discovery, CMDB validation, VMware assessment, SAP dependency mapping, TCO modeling, migration-wave planning, and DR validation. This mirrors DEAL-089’s win reason and DEAL-007’s accelerator-based win pattern.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so competitor pricing and actual award patterns cannot be validated against ground truth; confidence must be lower than if public awards were available.

Cognizant appears to be an existing strategic partner across data-center operations, cloud platforms, cybersecurity, observability, and end-user services, creating incumbent advantage and transition-friction risk.
The current RFP is Phase 0 only and does not guarantee downstream hosting, migration, or managed services revenue; the $15M–$50M estimate is inferred, not committed.

Kohler has hard eliminatory gates around timeline, designated communication channel, detailed template completion, compliance certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, and USD pricing; any miss can disqualify the bid regardless of solution strength.
Pricing risk is material: historical DEAL-029 shows technically stronger bids can lose when priced above market; Phase 0 and downstream scenarios must be defensible and transparent.

The RFP likely compares very different supplier types—hyperscalers, DCaaS providers, incumbent service providers, and global SIs—so evaluation may favor providers with direct hosting assets or entrenched operational relationships.
Without Kohler’s detailed inventory, cost baseline, application dependencies, RTO/RPO, and network/security tooling data, the business case may be perceived as assumption-heavy.
Net-90 or other commercial terms may pressure margin and require approval before bid submission, as flagged in the Kohler-specific proposal intelligence.
must_fix_before_bidding: Secure and fully populate Kohler’s required Vendor Response template with detailed requirement-by-requirement answers; no generic or Yes/No responses.

Attach a complete certification and compliance evidence matrix, including ISO/SOC/security attestations, hosting-location details, hyperscaler certifications, and explicit DCaaS provider attestations if DCaaS is included.
Confirm submission logistics before 26 June: correct designated channel, Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com, copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com, Q&A template compliance, and USD-only pricing.

Obtain or validate Kohler-specific inventory assumptions for SAP, VMware, physical servers, Windows/Linux, databases, backup/DR, networking, monitoring/logging, security tooling, RTO/RPO, and current cost baseline; otherwise clearly state assumptions and limits.

Build a priced scenario model for Phase 0 and downstream options: retained Wisconsin DC, DCaaS, public cloud, hybrid, SAP/non-SAP treatment, migration waves, run costs, transition costs, FinOps savings, and risk/rollback costs.
Lock in partner attestations and delivery commitments for any DCaaS, colocation, hyperscaler, backup/DR, or migration tooling partners before final submission.
Include a two-week mobilisation plan with named roles, manufacturing/SAP/hybrid-cloud SMEs, governance cadence, and executive decision checkpoints.

draft.rfp_id: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary:
Kohler’s Project Polaris should not be treated as a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise. Eric Tabor’s stated priorities for operational and business excellence through platforms, digital engineering and domain expertise, faster response to business needs, and a resilient global digital core point to a different decision: where each workload should run, how it should be governed, and how Kohler can modernize without disrupting manufacturing, SAP/ERP, hospitality, connected products, or customer-facing experiences.

HCLTech proposes a vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower, beginning with a contractually independent 12-week Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab and extending, if Kohler elects, into a 36-month co-managed hybrid modernization program. We will baseline workloads, dependencies, costs, service levels, security controls, IAM, VMware and SAP dependencies, RTO/RPO, observability, contracts, and operational ownership. We will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, plant/edge, hybrid, and SaaS integration options using open-book USD TCO and FinOps assumptions.

Our differentiator is simple: Kohler owns the evidence. The Phase 0 package includes workload placement scorecards, cloud-vs-DCaaS-vs-hybrid economics, compliance evidence matrix, DR/security/observability target architecture, vendor coexistence RACI, migration waves, rollback gates, and an implementation-ready downstream RFP any provider can bid on. With a no-pull-through guarantee or pre-disclosed conflict waiver, Polaris becomes a defensible hosting decision factory, not a sales funnel. This protects continuity while giving Kohler the confidence to scale its digital core.
win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Factory: evidence-led workload placement across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS, not a forced cloud-first migration.

Manufacturing-safe transformation with zero-disruption governance: plant and business continuity, SAP uptime, rollback gates, DR testing, resilience tiers, and fee-at-risk governance tied to availability.
Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: scenario-based 3–5 year economics for cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, SAP/non-SAP, licensing, migration, run, and support.

Compliance-first proposal discipline: visible certifications, attestations where applicable, hosting locations, data residency, security controls, RFP template compliance, communication channels, and USD pricing.

Rapid mobilisation plus accelerators: two-week mobilisation and named accelerators for discovery, CMDB validation, VMware assessment, SAP dependency mapping, TCO modeling, migration-wave planning, and DR validation.

sections.content:
Project Polaris sits at the intersection of infrastructure economics, resilience, security, and business speed. Kohler must evaluate hosting choices across data centers, cloud platforms, SaaS, enterprise systems, plant/edge environments, and incumbent operations without assuming that one venue is right for every workload. SAP and ERP services, manufacturing operations, hospitality platforms, analytics, connected products, IAM, observability, backup, DR, and customer experiences each carry different latency, compliance, RTO/RPO, integration, and cost profiles.

The challenge is therefore a governed placement decision, not a generic migration. Kohler needs evidence that withstands executive, finance, audit, security, and operations review: transparent USD economics; documented hosting locations and data-residency controls; identity-first access design; application availability safeguards; rollback gates; and a vendor coexistence model for Kohler, Cognizant, HCLTech, and other providers. Our approach keeps production continuity first, so Polaris can create faster business response and digital-core resilience without creating hidden operating risk.

HCLTech proposes the Balanced Hybrid Control Tower: a vendor-neutral decision factory followed by pragmatic co-managed modernization where Kohler chooses to proceed. The 12-week Hosting Decision Lab will produce Kohler-owned discovery data, CMDB validation, VMware assessment, SAP and non-SAP dependency maps, workload placement scorecards, and an open-book TCO model comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration patterns.

The target state is Azure-first where evidence supports it, not cloud-forced. We will design landing-zone governance with subscription/account structure, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, Entra ID integration, privileged-access governance, network segmentation, security logging, backup, DR, and observability/AIOps integration. Workloads will be assigned resilience tiers, FinOps guardrails, licensing assumptions, migration waves, and rollback gates. The result is an implementation-ready architecture and downstream RFP that any provider can execute, preserving choice while accelerating a manufacturing-safe hybrid future.

We will mobilize in two weeks with a Polaris command cell, confirmed stakeholders, data requests, inventory feeds, security access, governance cadence, and communication channels. Weeks 1–2 establish the current-state baseline across data centers, cloud, VMware, SAP/ERP, network, IAM, monitoring, backup, contracts, SLAs, and cost sources. Weeks 2–5 validate CMDB data and map application, infrastructure, SaaS, plant/edge, and operational dependencies.

Weeks 5–7 define hosting options and placement criteria. Weeks 7–9 build the open-book USD TCO, FinOps scenarios, reserved-capacity and licensing assumptions, migration and run-cost comparisons, and risk sensitivity analysis. Weeks 9–11 design the landing zone, security, DR test blueprint, observability/AIOps model, RACI, and transition controls. Week 12 produces the executive decision package. The 36-month program then executes approved waves with hypercare, rollback gates, resilience testing, and fee-at-risk governance tied to availability and disruption avoidance.

Polaris will be led by a senior HCLTech hosting strategy partner supported by architects for hybrid cloud, VMware, SAP dependencies, network, IAM, security, observability, FinOps, service management, and transition governance. The team will include discovery analysts to validate CMDB and inventory data, application dependency specialists for SAP and non-SAP mapping, DR and resilience leads, and a commercial modeler responsible for the USD TCO workbook and scenario assumptions.

We will work through an integrated Kohler/HCLTech governance model rather than an isolated consulting track. HCLTech’s relevant knowledge base includes manufacturing-safe transition patterns, accelerator-led discovery and migration planning, Azure landing-zone and monitoring experience, vulnerability-management workflow automation, SAP low-downtime lessons, and multi-provider operating-model design. This blend gives Kohler domain fluency, platform expertise, and disciplined proposal compliance, while preserving vendor neutrality through Kohler-owned artifacts and the no-pull-through decision package.

Choose HCLTech because our proposal aligns to the decision Kohler must make, not the downstream revenue others may prefer. We are prepared to deliver Phase 0 as a contractually independent fiduciary package: Kohler-owned data, transparent models, compliance evidence, target architecture, DR and observability blueprints, operating RACI, and a downstream RFP any qualified provider can bid on. If HCLTech participates later, it will do so only under a pre-disclosed conflict waiver.

That independence is paired with execution practicality. The recommended 36-month, $30,667,392 Hybrid Control Tower includes the decision lab, Azure-first governance where appropriate, co-managed migration waves, manufacturing hypercare, FinOps transparency, identity-first security, AIOps-enabled reliability, and fee-at-risk controls tied to availability and disruption avoidance. Kohler receives both strategic freedom and operational confidence: the ability to choose the right hosting pattern, prove the economics, protect production, and scale the global digital core with resilience.

critical_issues: The win-probability rationale relies heavily on DEAL-089, DEAL-003, DEAL-011, DEAL-007, DEAL-056, and DEAL-029, but none of these deal records appear in the supplied raw data. These should not be used as evidence.

Most competitor-specific evidence is unsupported: the raw data contains no Infosys, Accenture, Capgemini, or TCS financial extracts, employee counts, operating margins, bookings, job postings, Wisconsin hiring evidence, acquisitions, or recent win records.

The competitor price ranges are not validated by public contracts, cloud pricing details, or supplied financial data. They are model estimates only and should be presented, if at all, as low-confidence assumptions.
The statement that Accenture has Q3 FY2026 managed-services revenue of $9.39B, bookings of $19.32B, and 104 $100M+ bookings YTD is unsupported by the supplied sources.

The RFP compliance-gate claims, including eliminatory certification requirements, DCaaS attestation requirements, communication-channel gates, mandatory template rules, and rejection of Yes/No answers, are not present in the supplied raw data.

The raw procurement data exists but is unrelated to Kohler and the named competitors. The agent's wording that no public procurement data is available is too broad; the supported statement is only that no relevant Kohler/named-competitor procurement award data was provided.
The Kohler Project Polaris proposal record is directly relevant, but it has no stated outcome. It cannot be treated as proof of prior win performance or win-rate strength.

The killer differentiator is directionally aligned with the vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab concept, but the no-pull-through guarantee, downstream award bar/conflict-waiver construct, and claim that competitors cannot credibly match it are not supported by raw data.

Capability gaps tied to actual RFP requirements should be narrowed. Several listed gaps are plausible delivery risks, but the raw data does not verify them as mandatory or eliminatory RFP requirements.
